chunk_summary: This section discusses anthropometric methods and borderline malnutrition interpretation in outpatient care, focusing on moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) and supplementary feeding strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of anthropometry in diagnosing malnutrition in outpatient settings.
Identify criteria for moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) and appropriate interventions.
Learn supplementary feeding guidelines for borderline malnutrition cases.
key_facts: Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) is defined by weight-for-height Z-scores between -2 and -3 SD or mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) of 11.5–12.5 cm.
Supplementary feeding programs aim to provide 500–1000 kcal/day to address nutritional deficits.
Anthropometric measurements, such as MUAC and weight-for-height, are critical for monitoring malnutrition in outpatient care.
Borderline malnutrition cases require close monitoring to prevent progression to severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Outpatient care focuses on community-based management of malnutrition using accessible and cost-effective interventions.
reference_quote: Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) is managed through supplementary feeding programs providing 500–1000 kcal/day to prevent progression to severe malnutrition.
